## Introduction: why learn rewards? {.nav-intro}

Reinforcement learning (RL) has yielded high performing robotic control.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
```{=html}
<video autoplay loop muted playsinline style="width: 100%; height: 200px; object-fit: cover; object-position: center;">
  <source src="videos/humanoid-run.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
```
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
```{=html}
<div style="overflow: hidden; height: 200px;">
<video autoplay loop muted playsinline style="width: 100%; height: 250px; object-fit: cover; object-position: center; transform: scale(1.5); transform-origin: center center;">
  <source src="videos/bruce_swing_arms_smooth.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
</div>
```
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
```{=html}
<video autoplay loop muted playsinline style="width: 100%; height: 200px; object-fit: cover; object-position: center;">
  <source src="videos/inhand_z.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
```
:::
::::

Yet, process hinges on having a well-defined *reward function*.

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Reward Function"}
The reward function $R : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ maps (state, action) pairs to a scalar signal that is maximized when the agent achieves its *goal*. 
The reward function could also encode *how* that behavior is achieved.
:::

[*Not all objectives are easily describable via hand-crafted rewards.*]{style="display: block; text-align: center;;"}
